draft.introduction: Book XI of Apuleius’s Metamorphoses (The Golden Ass) describes the protagonist Lucius’s salvation through initiation into the mysteries of Isis. The goddess appears in a vision of overwhelming majesty and reveals herself as the universal divine feminine.

translation.text: Behold, Lucius, I am here, moved by your prayers—I, nature’s mother, mistress of all the elements, first-born of the ages, highest of divinities, queen of the dead, first of the celestials, the single form of all the gods and goddesses. The luminous heights of heaven, the healthful breezes of the sea, the mournful silences of the underworld—all these I govern at my will.
translation.original_text: En adsum tuis commota, Luci, precibus, rerum naturae parens, elementorum omnium domina, saeculorum progenies initialis, summa numinum, regina manium, prima caelitum.

translator_notes.note: Isis’s self-revelation as the “single form” of all deities reflects the henotheistic tendency of late Roman religion, in which many gods are understood as aspects of one supreme divinity. This passage influenced Neoplatonic theology and the concept of divine unity underlying multiplicity.
